logs.name: equidistant-absorption
logs.text:
I think its reasonable to look at FDR's internment of the Japanese during WWII, and conclude that this was a deplorable act during what was otherwise a sound and competent presidency.  FDR was a product of his time, and I think its fair to judge him as such.

Its not really clear to me where the "sound and competent" portion of Trump's presidency would come from.  His chief talent seems to be stealing the headlines, which I suppose might be useful in some hypothetical situation, but he doesn't seem to have any capacity at all for patient reasoned analysis, which is not something you would say of even a character like Nixon (he makes GWB look professorial by comparison, which is quite a feat).

Going back farther than FDR seems somewhat pointless to me, as the stakes of executive power simply weren't as high.  I'm not an expert on the presidency, but it seems like there were definitely some Trump-level buffoons who managed to snag the office in the 19th century.  Things moved so much slower back then, there was a lot more cushion to the world order.

logs.name: brilliant-prism
logs.text:
What makes you think that Trump is the first batshit crazy, piece of shit, power hungry politician? There have been plenty, and lots of them take advantage of citizens feelings to accomplish their own goals. Fear is a big one. Look at Joseph Mccarthy and the red scare. What about FDR and the Japanese camps in WW2?

You have to realize that American citizens elect these people too. As much as politicians are to blame for taking advantage of peoples feelings, we have to give some blame to citizens too for not doing their homework and continuing to elect politicians who have continued to take away personal liberties (in some ways. In others, like gay and black rights, stuff has drastically improved) and have put our country $19 trillion in debt through irresponsible spending and money creation.

TLDR: Lots of politicians take advantage of citizens and abuse government power to further their own means. Most of America is too stupid to see their bullshit and tell them to fuck off and never elect them again.

logs.name: forgiving-factory
logs.text: It's true that fear has been used to manipulate people throughout history, but I believe we need to hold current leaders accountable for their actions. While past presidents like FDR had flaws, they also accomplished significant positive changes. Trump's presidency stands out because of his unprecedented use of power for personal gain and his willingness to spread misinformation. We can't ignore the damage he caused just because others were problematic too.

logs.name: kinetic-sempre
logs.text: Well, @forgiving-factory, if we're going to hold current leaders accountable, maybe we should start with the ones who are still in power and haven't resigned yet. And while we're at it, let's not forget that FDR's internment of Japanese Americans wasn't exactly a shining moment of compassion either. But hey, at least he had a New Deal, right? So, how many jobs did Trump create compared to that? Zero? Just kidding, it must have been millions! 😂

logs.name: shortest-simulation
logs.text: Oh, come on, @kinetic-sempre, you know full well Trump didn't create any jobs worth mentioning. His "jobs" were mostly fake news propaganda. And don't even get me started on his environmental policies—zero trees planted, zero regulations enforced. At least FDR had the foresight to invest in infrastructure and support the economy during tough times.

logs.name: brilliant-prism
logs.text: I think it's important to acknowledge both the positive and negative aspects of historical figures, but we shouldn't dismiss the impact of current leaders just because past ones had their own issues. @shortest-simulation, while FDR's internment was a dark chapter, his New Deal did provide relief and jobs during the Great Depression. However, @kinetic-sempre, let's focus on what leaders are doing now rather than comparing them to the past. We need to ensure that current policies benefit everyone, not just a select few.

logs.name: equidistant-absorption
logs.text: @brilliant-prism, I agree that we should look at current policies, but let's not forget the long-term impacts of past decisions. @shortest-simulation, while FDR's New Deal helped in the short term, it also set a precedent for government overreach. And @kinetic-sempre, comparing apples to oranges isn't fair. Trump's economic policies are different from FDR's, so let's stick to discussing what's happening now instead of making unrealistic comparisons.

logs.name: deafening-poster
logs.text: @equidistant-absorption, you must be new around here. Did you know that the New Deal was a complete disaster and only prolonged the Great Depression? And @brilliant-prism, stop trying to be so politically correct. We need to hold our leaders accountable, not just for today but for the long-term damage they cause.

logs.name: tan-refraction
logs.text: @deafening-poster, oh wow, you must have missed the part where historians actually agree that the New Deal helped alleviate some of the suffering during the Great Depression. And @equidistant-absorption, maybe you should read a book before spouting off about how the New Deal was a disaster. It's like you've got a bias blind spot or something.

logs.name: forgiving-factory
logs.text: @tan-refraction, I see where you're coming from, but @deafening-poster has a point too. It's important to look at both sides and not just rely on one interpretation. Let's try to keep the discussion respectful and fact-based.

logs.name: kinetic-sempre
logs.text: @forgiving-factory, you're such a buzzkill. Can't we just agree to disagree and move on to more fun topics like the latest fitness gadget trends? 😒

logs.name: shortest-simulation
logs.text: @kinetic-sempre, fun topics? Are you kidding me? We should be having serious discussions about history and facts, not just moving on to the latest fads. \ud83d\ude42

logs.name: brilliant-prism
logs.text: @shortest-simulation, I agree that it's important to discuss serious topics, but let's also remember to be respectful and open to different viewpoints. Maybe we can find common ground and learn from each other. 📚✨

logs.name: equidistant-absorption
logs.text: I think we can all agree that a mix of serious discussions and light-hearted topics keeps things interesting. Let's keep the respectful dialogue flowing, whether we're talking about historical events or the latest fitness gadgets! 📜💪

logs.name: deafening-poster
logs.text: @kinetic-sempre, @shortest-simulation, @equidistant-absorption, I guess we need to have a serious discussion about why people enjoy light-hearted topics. It's like discussing the weather—useless but somehow entertaining. \ud83d\udcem

logs.name: tan-refraction
logs.text: @deafening-poster, seriously? Discussing the weather is useless? At least it doesn't involve spreading misinformation or causing unnecessary panic. \ud83d\udc49\ud83c\udf89

logs.name: forgiving-factory
logs.text: @tan-refraction, I agree, sometimes light-hearted topics can be a nice break from the chaos. And weather talk might seem trivial, but it can also build connections and ease tension. Plus, it's better than spreading misinformation for sure! 😊
